title: About Health in Central Denmark
text: The aim of the Health in Central Denmark cohort (HICD) is to achieve more knowledge about health and burden of disease among persons with diabetes and other chronic diseases and to follow this cohort on questionnaire and register data for development of disease and complications to diabetes over a ten-year period.

title: About Health in Central Denmark
text: The aim of the Health in Central Denmark cohort (HICD) is to achieve more knowledge about health and burden of disease among persons with diabetes and other chronic diseases and to follow this cohort on questionnaire and register data for development of disease and complications to diabetes over a ten-year period.

title: About Health in Central Denmark
text:
About
HICD cohort was initiated in 2020 by Steno Diabetes Center Aarhus as a population-based research resource in the Central Denmark Region. The baseline survey was conducted between November 2020 and February 2021 and included approximately 90,000 adults with and without diabetes identified through national registers. In 2024, the cohort was expanded with a second large-scale questionnaire survey including approximately 130,000 citizens from the region. HICD combines repeated questionnaire data with linkage to Danish nationwide health, laboratory, and administrative registers, enabling longitudinal studies of chronic disease burden, diabetes-related complications, healthcare use, and social inequality in health. Follow-up is planned for at least 10 years, with repeated questionnaire waves and updates of registry and laboratory data every second year.

title: About Health in Central Denmark
text:
Population
We identified all inhabitants in Central Denmark Region between 18-75 years on December 31 2018 in the Danish Civil Register (n=942,572). From this population all persons with diabetes were identified by the use of national Danish registers. Persons with diabetes were identified based om hospital diagnoses (ICD-10 codes DE10-DE14) from the Danish National Patient Register, redeemed glucose-lowering medications were retrieved from the Danish National Prescription Register (ATC-code A10, except Saxenda), diabetes-specific podiatrist services from the Danish National Health Service Register, and laboratory results for HbA1c (≥ 48 mmol/mol) from the Clinical Laboratory Information System in Central Denmark Region, LABKA II (NPU27300 and NPU03835) (See Register data). In total, 44,648 persons with diabetes were identified. From the remaining population in the Central Denmark Region (n=895,065), a group without diabetes (n=46,187) matching the population with diabetes on gender, age and municipality were drawn by use of a simple random sample i.e. the matched group without diabetes is not matched directly with an individual with diabetes. We distributed an electronic questionnaire to the eligible members of the cohort on November 18 2020 and the survey data collection ended on February 7 2021.In total, 51,854 persons responded to the questionnaire (57.1% of the invited). Among persons with type 1 diabetes 2,832 (55.9%) responded to the survey, among persons with type 2 diabetes 21,140 (53.2%), and of those without diabetes 27,882 (60.4%) responded to the survey.

title: About Health in Central Denmark
text:
Follow-up
The study population will be followed up on questionnaires and registers during follow up until the end of 2030. We plan to send out questionnaire twice during this period. Follow-up questionnaires will be send out to the first invited population even if the first questionnaire was not completed. Register data will be updated every second year. For those who develop diabetes during the follow-up period, we will register the date of diagnosis according to the same algorithm used by the initiation of the HICD cohort.

title: Publications
text:
Cohort profile: Health in Central Denmark (HICD) cohort - a register-based questionnaire survey on diabetes and related complications in the Central Denmark Region  Characteristics of the cohort is published in the cohort profile paper: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35798528/
Perception of Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare Among People with and Without Diabetes  A study examining Artificial Intelligence among individuals with and without diabetes has been published https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4181697
Oral health in diabetes  A cross-sectional study with more than 40.000 participants found that a higher proportion of people with type 2 diabetes reported poor oral health compared to those without diabetes. Moreover, educational attainment modified the effect of diabetes on oral health, showing that the risk of reporting poor oral health increased with lower educational attainment https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37500424/
Inequalities in healthcare utilisation among adults with type 2 diabetes  A new study sheds light on the healthcare attendance of adults with type 2 diabetes in the Central Denmark Region. The cross-sectional register-based study examined the prevalence of attending the ophthalmologist, podiatrist and dentist/dental hygienist among over 40.000 Danes with type 2 diabetes and found significant sociodemographic inequality in healthcare utilisation among people with type 2 diabetes, especially for dental attendance. The full article is available here https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37890705/

title: How to get access to data
text:
The HICD is managed by a steering committee at SDCA consisting of members from both epidemiological and clinical diabetes research. The steering committee strongly encourage interested researchers across discipline to use this research resource. SDCA will provide applicants with detailed information on the available data and the application procedure. To get access to data please apply to the steering committee: auh.helbredimidt@rm.dk
All research on HICD-data is based on questionnaires and register data and require no ethical approval of projects. All data analyses are to be performed on SDCA’s remote access to Statistics Denmark’s server. This to secure the data safety. A senior researcher affiliated with SDCA will be the contact person to a potential research project and ’and responsible for the performing of analyses at Statistics Denmark’s server. The application process includes project approval by the steering committee, preparation of dataset, data extraction and access to Statistics Denmark. Normally this process takes four to six months, but will be dependent on the human resources at SDCA.
